title: A Debate About Screen Time
summary: A parent and her teenage son debate how much time he should spend on his phone, exploring concerns about health, social life, and personal responsibility.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: Jake, I've been wanting to talk to you about how much time you spend on your phone every day.

body.speaker: Teen
body.text: Here we go again. Mum, I'm not a little kid anymore. I'm fifteen.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: I know how old you are. But last week your screen time app said you averaged six hours a day. That's a lot.

body.speaker: Teen
body.text: That includes school stuff too, like research and online lessons. It's not all gaming and videos.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: Even so, I'm worried about your sleep. You were up past midnight on Tuesday, and you were exhausted the next morning.

body.speaker: Teen
body.text: That was because I was talking to Leo about a group project. It was actually important.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: I understand that friendships matter. But there has to be a limit. Studies show that screens before bed make it harder to fall asleep.

body.speaker: Teen
body.text: Mum, everything causes problems according to some study. Sitting causes problems. Eating causes problems. I can't just switch off.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: I'm not asking you to switch off completely. I'm suggesting a rule: no screens after ten o'clock on school nights.

body.speaker: Teen
body.text: That's way too early. What if someone messages me about homework at ten fifteen?

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: Then you can reply in the morning. The world won't end if you take eight hours off your phone.

body.speaker: Teen
body.text: Fine, but what about weekends? You can't apply the same rule to Saturdays.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: On weekends, eleven would be fine. But you also need to spend some time away from screens entirely — outdoors or just reading.

body.speaker: Teen
body.text: I do go out. I went cycling with Danny last Saturday. You just didn't notice because you weren't home.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: That's fair, and I'm glad you did. I'm not saying you never go out. I just think balance is important.

body.speaker: Teen
body.text: OK, what if we try ten thirty on school nights for two weeks and see how it goes? If my grades stay up and I'm not tired, can we revisit it?

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: That sounds reasonable. I appreciate that you're willing to compromise rather than just argue.

body.speaker: Teen
body.text: I'm not unreasonable. I just think rules should make sense, not just exist because that's the way it's always been.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: Fair point. Let's try ten thirty and check in after two weeks. Deal?

body.speaker: Teen
body.text: Deal. But you have to admit when I'm right if my grades actually improve.

annotations.meaningVi: Lại bắt đầu rồi (diễn đạt sự chán nản khi điều gì đó lặp lại)
annotations.exampleEn: Oh, here we go again — another meeting about the same problem.

annotations.meaningVi: trung bình, tính ra trung bình là
annotations.exampleEn: He averaged eight hours of sleep a night throughout the month.
The weather was bad; even so, they decided to go hiking.
After the long hike, she was completely exhausted.

annotations.phrase: there has to be a limit
annotations.exampleEn: You can work hard, but there has to be a limit to avoid burning out.

annotations.meaningVi: tắt đi, nghỉ ngơi hoàn toàn (không dùng thiết bị)
annotations.exampleEn: She finds it hard to switch off after a busy day at work.

annotations.meaningVi: áp dụng cùng một quy tắc
annotations.exampleEn: You can't apply the same rule to every situation — context matters.

annotations.meaningVi: sự cân bằng là quan trọng
annotations.exampleEn: When it comes to diet and exercise, balance is important.

annotations.meaningVi: xem xét lại, thảo luận lại
annotations.exampleEn: Let's revisit this decision after we see the results.

annotations.meaningVi: sẵn sàng nhân nhượng, chịu thoả hiệp
annotations.exampleEn: A good negotiator is always willing to compromise.
His demands were completely unreasonable given the circumstances.

annotations.meaningVi: kiểm tra lại, hỏi thăm tiến độ
annotations.exampleEn: Let's check in next week to see how the plan is working.

annotations.meaningVi: thời gian dùng màn hình thiết bị điện tử
annotations.exampleEn: Experts recommend limiting children's screen time to two hours a day.

annotations.meaningVi: điểm số vẫn cao, kết quả học tập không giảm
annotations.exampleEn: She kept her grades up even while working part-time.
The new timetable doesn't make sense — the lunch break is too short.

grammarNotes.title: First Conditional — Talking about possible future situations
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Hội thoại có nhiều câu điều kiện loại 1 để thảo luận về các tình huống có thể xảy ra: 'If my grades stay up and I'm not tired, can we revisit it?', 'What if someone messages me about homework at ten fifteen?'. Cấu trúc: If + present simple, will/can/modal + infinitive. Loại điều kiện này dùng để nói về kết quả thực tế có thể xảy ra trong tương lai.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses first conditional structures to discuss possible future situations: 'If my grades stay up and I'm not tired, can we revisit it?', 'What if someone messages me about homework at ten fifteen?' Form: If + present simple, will/can/modal + infinitive. This type of conditional is used for realistic future possibilities.

grammarNotes.title: Negative Structures for Partial Disagreement
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Các nhân vật dùng cấu trúc phủ định một phần để tránh mâu thuẫn hoàn toàn: 'I'm not asking you to switch off completely', 'I'm not saying you never go out', 'I'm not unreasonable'. Cấu trúc 'not ... completely' và 'not ... never' giúp thừa nhận một phần của lập luận đối phương trong khi vẫn giữ vững quan điểm của mình. Đây là kỹ năng quan trọng trong hội thoại lịch sự và thương lượng.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The speakers use partial negative structures to avoid total disagreement: 'I'm not asking you to switch off completely', 'I'm not saying you never go out', 'I'm not unreasonable'. The patterns 'not...completely' and 'not...never' acknowledge part of the other person's point while maintaining one's own position — an important skill in polite conversation and negotiation.

translationVi:
Mẹ: Jake, mẹ muốn nói chuyện với con về việc con dành bao nhiêu thời gian trên điện thoại mỗi ngày.

Con: Lại bắt đầu rồi. Mẹ ơi, con không còn là đứa trẻ nữa. Con đã mười lăm tuổi rồi.

Mẹ: Mẹ biết con bao nhiêu tuổi. Nhưng tuần trước ứng dụng theo dõi thời gian màn hình của con nói con trung bình sáu tiếng một ngày. Nhiều lắm đấy.

Con: Bao gồm cả việc học ở trường nữa mẹ ơi, như nghiên cứu và bài học trực tuyến. Không phải toàn chơi game và xem video đâu.

Mẹ: Dù vậy, mẹ lo cho giấc ngủ của con. Thứ Ba tuần rồi con thức đến tận nửa đêm, và sáng hôm sau con kiệt sức.

Con: Đó là vì con đang nói chuyện với Leo về dự án nhóm. Thực ra quan trọng lắm đấy.

Mẹ: Mẹ hiểu tình bạn quan trọng. Nhưng phải có giới hạn chứ. Các nghiên cứu cho thấy nhìn màn hình trước khi ngủ khiến khó ngủ hơn.

Con: Mẹ ơi, theo một nghiên cứu nào đó thì cái gì cũng gây ra vấn đề. Ngồi gây vấn đề. Ăn gây vấn đề. Con không thể cứ tắt máy đi được.

Mẹ: Mẹ không yêu cầu con tắt hoàn toàn. Mẹ đề xuất một quy tắc: không dùng màn hình sau mười giờ tối vào các tối đi học.

Con: Sớm quá đi. Nếu ai nhắn tin hỏi về bài tập lúc mười giờ mười lăm thì sao?

Mẹ: Thì con có thể trả lời vào buổi sáng. Trái đất sẽ không nổ tung nếu con nghỉ điện thoại tám tiếng đâu.

Con: Thôi được, nhưng còn cuối tuần thì sao? Mẹ không thể áp dụng quy tắc đó cho ngày thứ Bảy được.

Mẹ: Cuối tuần thì mười một giờ được rồi. Nhưng con cũng cần dành một khoảng thời gian hoàn toàn xa màn hình — ra ngoài hoặc chỉ đọc sách thôi.

Con: Con có ra ngoài mà. Con đi đạp xe với Danny thứ Bảy tuần trước. Mẹ chỉ không để ý vì mẹ không có ở nhà.

Mẹ: Điều đó có lý đấy, và mẹ mừng vì con đã làm vậy. Mẹ không nói con không bao giờ ra ngoài. Mẹ chỉ nghĩ sự cân bằng là quan trọng.

Con: OK, nếu chúng ta thử mười giờ rưỡi vào các tối đi học trong hai tuần xem sao? Nếu điểm của con vẫn tốt và con không mệt mỏi, chúng ta có thể xem xét lại không?

Mẹ: Nghe có vẻ hợp lý đấy. Mẹ biết ơn vì con sẵn sàng thoả hiệp thay vì chỉ tranh luận.

Con: Con không phải người vô lý. Con chỉ nghĩ các quy tắc phải có lý, chứ không chỉ tồn tại vì đó là cách mọi thứ vẫn luôn được làm.

Mẹ: Điểm đó đúng đấy. Hãy thử mười giờ rưỡi và kiểm tra lại sau hai tuần nhé. Đồng ý chứ?

Con: Đồng ý. Nhưng mẹ phải thừa nhận khi con đúng nếu điểm của con thực sự cải thiện.

questions.prompt: What did Jake's screen time app show about his usage last week?
options: He used his phone for two hours a day
He averaged six hours a day
He spent more than eight hours a day on screens
He had reduced his screen time significantly
questions.explanation: The parent says 'last week your screen time app said you averaged six hours a day.'
questions.hint: The parent refers to a specific app measurement.

questions.prompt: Why does Jake say some of his screen time is necessary?
options: Because he has to watch educational videos for sport
Because it includes school research and online lessons
Because his teachers require him to be online at all times
Because he works a part-time job online
questions.explanation: Jake says 'That includes school stuff too, like research and online lessons.'
questions.hint: Jake defends his usage by mentioning academic activities.

questions.prompt: What was Jake doing on Tuesday night that kept him up past midnight?
options: Playing an online game with friends
Talking to Leo about a group project
questions.explanation: Jake says 'That was because I was talking to Leo about a group project.'

questions.prompt: What rule does the parent initially propose for school nights?
options: Phones must be handed in at bedtime
questions.explanation: The parent proposes 'no screens after ten o'clock on school nights.'
questions.hint: The parent states a specific time.

questions.prompt: What time does the parent suggest for weekends?
questions.explanation: The parent says 'On weekends, eleven would be fine.'
questions.hint: The parent agrees to a later time for non-school nights.

questions.prompt: What did Jake do last Saturday that the parent had not noticed?
options: He went to the library to study
He went cycling with his friend Danny
He played football with friends at the park
questions.explanation: Jake says 'I went cycling with Danny last Saturday.'
questions.hint: Jake gives a specific example of outdoor activity.

options: He will put his phone away at ten o'clock from now on
He will try ten thirty on school nights for two weeks
He will only use his phone for school work on weekdays
He will reduce his daily screen time by half
questions.explanation: Jake proposes 'try ten thirty on school nights for two weeks and see how it goes.'
questions.hint: Jake suggests a trial period at a middle-ground time.

questions.prompt: What condition does Jake attach to the compromise?
options: That the rule will not apply during exam periods
That weekends have no restrictions at all
That they can revisit the rule if his grades stay up and he is not tired
That his parent will also limit her screen time
questions.explanation: Jake says 'If my grades stay up and I'm not tired, can we revisit it?'
questions.hint: Jake links the outcome to two measurable conditions.

questions.prompt: How does the parent describe Jake's approach to the compromise?
options: Reasonable and showing willingness to compromise
questions.explanation: The parent says 'That sounds reasonable. I appreciate that you're willing to compromise rather than just argue.'
questions.hint: The parent uses a positive word to describe Jake's suggestion.

questions.prompt: What does Jake say about why rules should exist?
options: Rules should always come from parents, not teenagers
Rules should be as strict as possible to be effective
Rules should make sense, not just exist out of tradition
Rules should be agreed on by both parents and children equally
questions.explanation: Jake says 'I just think rules should make sense, not just exist because that's the way it's always been.'
questions.hint: Jake argues for the logic behind rules, not just habit.

questions.prompt: What does the parent say at the end to confirm the agreement?
options: She says she will discuss it with Jake's father first
She says they will try ten thirty and check in after two weeks
She agrees to Jake's original suggestion of no restrictions
She says she needs more time to think about it
questions.explanation: The parent says 'Let's try ten thirty and check in after two weeks. Deal?'
questions.hint: The parent repeats the agreed time and sets a review date.

cloze.template: Parent: Jake, I've been wanting to talk to you about how much time you spend on your phone every day. Teen: Here we go again. Mum, I'm not a little kid anymore. I'm fifteen. Parent: I know how old you are. But last week your screen time app said you {{b1}} six hours a day. That's a lot. Teen: That includes school stuff too, like research and online lessons. It's not all gaming and videos. Parent: Even so, I'm worried about your sleep. You were up past midnight on Tuesday, and you were {{b2}} the next morning. Teen: That was because I was talking to Leo about a group project. It was actually important. Parent: I understand that friendships matter. But there has to be a limit. Studies show that screens before bed make it harder to {{b3}}. Teen: Mum, everything causes problems according to some study. I can't just {{b4}} completely. Parent: I'm not asking you to switch off completely. I'm suggesting a rule: no screens after ten o'clock on school nights. Teen: That's way too early. What if someone messages me about homework at ten fifteen? Parent: Then you can {{b5}} in the morning. The world won't end if you take eight hours off your phone. Teen: Fine, but what about weekends? You can't {{b6}} the same rule to Saturdays. Parent: On weekends, eleven would be fine. But you also need to spend some time away from screens entirely — outdoors or just reading. Teen: I do go out. I went {{b7}} with Danny last Saturday. You just didn't notice because you weren't home. Parent: That's fair, and I'm glad you did. I'm not saying you never go out. I just think {{b8}} is important. Teen: OK, what if we try ten thirty on school nights for two weeks and see how it goes? If my grades stay up and I'm not tired, can we {{b9}} it? Parent: That sounds reasonable. I appreciate that you're willing to {{b10}} rather than just argue. Teen: Deal. But you have to admit when I'm right if my grades actually improve.

blanks.explanation: 'You averaged six hours a day' — 'averaged' means the mean amount over a period of time, used here with screen time data.
'You were exhausted the next morning' — 'exhausted' is stronger than 'tired' and matches the parent's concern about sleep.
'Screens before bed make it harder to fall asleep' — 'fall asleep' is the natural phrase for transitioning from wakefulness to sleep.
'I can't just switch off' is the phrase — used figuratively to mean stopping all screen use, matching the parent's earlier suggestion.
'You can reply in the morning' — 'reply' is the most natural word for responding to a message.
'You can't apply the same rule to Saturdays' — 'apply' collocates with 'rule' in formal and informal contexts.
'I went cycling with Danny' — cycling is the specific activity Jake mentions as evidence of going outdoors.
'Balance is important' — the parent uses 'balance' as the key concept in the screen time argument.
'Can we revisit it?' — 'revisit' means to come back and reconsider a decision, a useful word in negotiation.
'Willing to compromise rather than just argue' — 'compromise' means reaching an agreement by each side giving up something.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Jake and his mother reach an agreement by negotiating rather than one person simply winning the argument. What skills did each of them use to reach a fair outcome? Do you think their agreement will work in the long term, and why?
